coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots can enable efficient photochemical interfacial activation of molecular oxygen in iron oxides/oxalate system
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yongjie
Huang, Mingjie
Xiang, Wei
Wu, Xiaohui
Zhou, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Photochemical reactions between natural iron oxides and oxalic acid (OA) have attracted wide attentions in environmental decontamination, but the critical interfacial ligand-to-metal charge transfer process (LMCT) is difficult to occur. In this study, carbon quantum dots modified maghemite (γ-Fe2O3/CQDs) with excellent photo-electrochemical properties has been successfully synthesized, which achieved rapid decomposition of sulfamethoxazole (SMX) with OA under UV. It was found that the complexed [≡FeIII(C2O4)n]3-2n on the γ-Fe2O3 surface could occur efficient interfacial LMCT, followed by fast internal transmission of electrons to CQDs part for activating its surface adsorbed O2 to efficiently produce O2 •− and •OH. The heterogeneous LMCT would be dominant in UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system for the initial rapid SMX degradation, despite the non-reductive leaching of aqueous FeIII(C2O4)n 3-2n would cause additional homogeneous LMCT. Bonded CQDs at iron oxides could highly improve utilization of the photo-generated electrons from complexed OA for O2 activation, and also broaden its application under visible light.

(a) Time-dependent SMX degradation and (b) simultaneous dissolved iron species in the four UV/IO/OA systems (γ-Fe2O3, HT/γ-Fe2O3, CQDs&γ-Fe2O3, and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs). Inset in Fig. 1a is the simultaneous OA consumption. Degradation of SMX in (c) the four UV/IOs/CQDs/OA systems (α-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3, Fe3O4 and γ-Fe2O3, with 0.05 mg/g CQDs), and (d) the four UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs /ligand systems. Experimental conditions: [SMX] = 10 mg/L, [IOs] = 0.1 g/L, [OA or ligand] = 0.2 mM, pH = 3.0 and temperature of 25 ℃.

Effects of (a) mass ratio of CQDs/γ-Fe2O3, initial dosages of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, (b) initial pH and OA, and (c) initial SMX concentration and reaction temperature on the kobs(SMX) in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system. Except the investigated factor, other experimental conditions were: [SMX] = 10 mg/L, [IOs] = 0.1 g/L, [OA] = 0.2 mM, pH = 3.0 and temperature of 25 ℃.

(a) VB-XPS spectra, and (b) UV–vis/DRS spectra of the γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs before and after 60 min pre-adsorption of OA. (c) The related valence band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM) potentials of the four samples.

High resolution XPS spectra of (a) O 1s, (b) C 1s, and (c) Fe 2p of the γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs before and after 60 min pre-adsorption of OA.

(a) EPR spectra of DMPO adducts, and (b) effects of the scavengers on the SMX degradation in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system. Experimental conditions: [SMX] = 10 mg/L, [γ-Fe2O3/CQDs] = 0.1 g/L, [OA] = 0.2 mM, pH = 3.0, and temperature of 25 ℃.

Evolution of dissolved iron in comparative systems under (a) UV irradiation and (b) dark; as well as SMX degradation in (c) the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA systems with pre-adsorption of OA or not, and (d) the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA and the UV/γ-Fe2O3/OA systems closed to air. Experimental conditions: [SMX] = 10 mg/L, [γ-Fe2O3/CQDs] = 0.1 g/L, [OA] = 0.2 mM, pH = 3.0, and temperature of 25 ℃.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photochemical reactions between natural iron oxides and oxalic acid (OA) have attracted wide attentions in environmental decontamination, but the critical interfacial ligand-to-metal charge transfer process (LMCT) is difficult to occur. In this study, carbon quantum dots modified maghemite (γ-Fe2O3/CQDs) with excellent photo-electrochemical properties has been successfully synthesized, which achieved rapid decomposition of sulfamethoxazole (SMX) with OA under UV. It was found that the complexed [≡FeIII(C2O4)n]3-2n on the γ-Fe2O3 surface could occur efficient interfacial LMCT, followed by fast internal transmission of electrons to CQDs part for activating its surface adsorbed O2 to efficiently produce O2 •− and •OH. The heterogeneous LMCT would be dominant in UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system for the initial rapid SMX degradation, despite the non-reductive leaching of aqueous FeIII(C2O4)n 3-2n would cause additional homogeneous LMCT. Bonded CQDs at iron oxides could highly improve utilization of the photo-generated electrons from complexed OA for O2 activation, and also broaden its application under visible light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of organic acid commonly secreted by plant roots in nature, oxalic acid (OA) can exhibit interesting photochemical behaviors connecting with iron ions, which have attracted wide attentions in potential environmental decontamination of aerosols, waters and soils [1]. The phenomenon of photo-induced reduction of ferrioxalate was first reported in 1953 [2], followed by studies revealing of similar photochemical activities in iron complexes bearing a carboxylate group in their ligand structures [3–5]. Fe(III)-OA complexes can be activated to occur ligand to metal charge transfer (LMCT) process under the irradiation of UV or sunlight, resulting in transient reduction of the iron center and generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as CO2 •−, •OH and O2 •−, which cause oxidative degradation of organic ligand and target organic pollutants [1,3,4,6]. Compared to conventional Fenton process (Fe2+/H2O2), the homogeneous photochemical Fe(III)-OA system appears to be of great significance, due to the green auto-oxidation process by replacing H2O2 and the photochemical efficient iron cycle for ROS generation [7].

In the past decades, heterogeneous photochemical iron-OA systems have been widely highlighted by utilizing of iron (hydr)oxides (IOs) available abundantly in natural environment [8]. Most natural IOs, e.g., goethite [9], magnetite [10], hematite [11], maghemite [12] and schwertmannite [13], were capable of degrading recalcitrant organic pollutants under UV irradiation. Unlike the homogeneous photochemical LMCT which can lead to fast intramolecular charge transfer from oxalate ligand to the Fe(III) center, the interfacial LMCT on the surface of IOs is generally difficult to occur [14,15]. Therefore, leaching of essential bulk Fe(III)-OA complexes to induce homogeneous LMCT is generally acknowledged as the main reaction mechanism [16]. The differences in the degradation efficiency could be mainly attributed to the dissimilar behaviors in the non-reductive complexing dissolution for types of IOs (e.g., crystal structure and particle size) [17]. As a result, sonochemical [18] and electrochemical [19] methods have been introduced in pursing enhancement effects, however, high cost and uncontrollable dissolved iron limited their potential applications.

Recently, the interfacial coordination and photochemical dissolution behaviors of OA upon IOs have been studied at the molecular level. Most studies have reported that the photocatalytic activity of IOs-OA systems was consistently highly dependent on the amount of surface monodentate binuclear inner spherical Fe(III)-OA complexes [4,6], demonstrating the occurrences of non-reduction processes [19,20] and absence of interfacial electron transfer of the complexed OA [2,5,8]. Besides, the homogeneous Fe(III)-OA complex may follow a photo-dissociation mechanism instead of LMCT, which could lead to efficient generation of two CO2 •− per Fe(III)-OA complex in the hematite-oxalate system [20]. In summary, realizing the efficient interfacial LMCT process, i.e. the photochemical electron transfer from the adsorbed oxalate to Fe(III) in the IOs particles, is still a great challenge due to the intrinsic photochemical properties of the IOs [17].

As a popular intensifying nanostructured form of carbon material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can exhibit extensive photonic and electronic properties, as well as their light-collecting, tunable/up-converted photoluminescence, and photo-induced transfer of electrons [21]. Owing to the excellent optical absorptivity, chemical stability, nontoxicity and facile synthesis [22], CQDs have great advantages in visible-light-induced catalytic applications for fully utilizing the energy from the solar spectrum [9,23], and multiple functions in photo-redox processes [24]. More important, CQDs possess the quantum confinement and edge effect as a result of HOMO-LUMO gap, which suggests the light harvesting ability as well as the possibility of electron injection from CQDs to semiconductors. In this connection, CQDs with matched HOMO-LUMO gap are expected to be combined with semi-conductive nanostructures as photoanodes for remarkable photoelectrochemical oxidation. Notably, with large π-conjugated structure, accordingly CQDs are highly electrically conductive, which facilitates charge carrier separation [25]. Meanwhile, CQDs can improve the utilization of photogenerated electrons and the electrons in CQDs are captured by O2 to produce O2 •− and the role of O2 •− in the photocatalytic process is significantly improved. This could be attributed to highly efficient charge separation and transfer efficiency induced by heterogeneous interfaces as well as multiphase-induced new electronic energy level structure [26]. Recently, Zhang et al. found that the p–p interaction between conjugated structure of CQDs and benzene could lead to the enrichment of benzene on the surface of Fe2O3/CQDs composite, resulting in an enhanced photocatalytic activity of the Fe2O3/CQDs nanocomposites for toxic gas (gas-phase benzene and methanol) degradation. Zeinab Rabiei et al. also found the suppressed electron-hole recombination, higher charge transfer rate and faster water oxidation kinetics at the surface of photoelectrode after CQD modification [27]. Thus, it can be speculated that the introduction of CQDs upon IOs catalysts would be beneficial to the photochemical reactions of the IOs-OA systems, however, the related study was scarcely.

Therefore, CQDs were successfully loaded at a typical IO maghemite in this study by adopting the simple common hydrothermal method. The synthesized γ-Fe2O3/CQDs could exhibit excellent rapid decomposition of antibiotic sulfamethoxazole (SMX) with oxalic acid (OA) under UV. The purpose of this study was to: (1) identify the excellent photo-electrochemical properties of the modified photocatalyst caused by the CQDs, (2) distinguish the heterogeneous and homogeneous reactions based on investigation of ROS generations and iron evolutions, and (3) reveal the improvement role of CQDs and propose a novel mechanism in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system based on the excited surface LMCT.

Chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade or higher. Acetonitrile and methanol were obtained from Fisher Scientific. Oxalic acid (H2C2O4·2H2O), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), glucose (C6H12O6), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid (CH3COOH), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HONH3Cl), thymine, ethanol, tert-butyl alcohol, p-benzoquinone, and 1,10-phenanthroline monohydrate (o-phen), catalase (CAT, 2000–5000 U/mg protein) were acquired from Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co. ltd., China. All solution were prepared in deionized water.

In a typical synthesis procedure, 0.8 g of glucose (as the precursor of carbon dots) was firstly dissolved in 50 mL mixture of alcohol-water with a volume ratio of 1.5:1. Then, 5.0 g γ-Fe2O3 was evenly dispersed in the solution and stirred for 12 h, and the suspension was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal reaction kettle. After heating at 180℃ for 24 h, the cooled precipitates were collected, followed by washing of deionized water and vacuum drying at 60℃. The obtained solids were grinded and the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs particles were finally prepared.

In a typical degradation experimental run, solution with certain concentrations of SMX/OA was put and magnetically stirred in a 500 mL jacket glass reactor equipped with a UV lamp inside, where the reaction temperature was kept constant at 25℃ by a low-temperature thermostatic bath. Initial pH was adjusted by 0.01 mol/L NaOH or H2SO4. The reaction was started by adding certain amounts of solid catalysts with simultaneous UV irradiation. At specified time intervals, water samples were taken from the reactor and filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane, with addition of 200 μL methanol (0.2 mol/L) to eliminate the subsequent degradation reaction. In the quenching experiments, predetermined concentrations of MeOH, EtOH, TBA and K2CrO7 were added before the reaction, respectively. All experiments in this study were carried out at duplicates.

Quantification of SMX was performed by a HPLC (Ultimate 3000, Thermo, USA), equipped with a C18 column and a UV detector. The mobile phase was a mixture of 1 % acetic acid (40 %) and acetonitrile (60 %), with a flow rate of 0.7 mL min−1. OA was measured using high performance anion exchange chromatography (Thermo ICS-900, USA). The mass ratio of carbon quantum dots loaded on the γ-Fe2O3 was measured by sulfur-carbon analyzer (EMIA Pro, Japan). Ferrous/total dissolved iron and H2O2 were spectrometrically analyzed by the 1,10-phenanthroline method and the DPD method, respectively [14]. Examination of the generated CO2 •−, •OH and O2 •− were conducted by a JES FA 200 X–band spectrometer (EPR, JEOL, Japan), adopting DMPO trapping agent (20 mM) at room temperature. The oxalic acid was quantified by an ion-chromatographic system (Dionex DX120) provided with an Ion Pac AS-14 column. Aqueous solutions of 3.5 mM Na2CO3 and 1.0 mM NaHCO3 were used as eluents and operated at isocratic mode with a flow rate of 1.2 mL/min for the ion-chromatographic system. The intermediates of SMZ were identified by HPLC-ESI-MS (Agilent 1100, USA).

All potentials were presented with reference to the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) scale. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the various iron oxides were characterized by a X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (Analytical B.V., Netherlands). Attenuated total reflection Flourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) spectra were collected by a Bruker VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) which equipped with an ATR accessory with single reflection. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an AXIS ULTRA DLD instrument (Shimadzu/Kratos, Japan), the binding energies of all peaks were referenced to the C 1 s line (285.0 eV). The a.c.-impedance spectra were measured by using a frequency response analyser (Solartron, SI 1255 FRA) in conjunction with a potentiostat (Solartron, SI 1287 ECI) with a 5 mV ac signal superimposed on an applied potential of 0.1 V (versus SCE) over the frequency range of 102 to 105 Hz. The anodic current transient was measured by application of a potential jump from 0.1 to 0.4 V (versus SCE) by using the potentiostat (Zahner IM6E impedance analyzer). The cyclic voltammetry measurement was performed in the potential range of 0–1.0 V (versus SCE) with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 using an EG&G PARC model 263 A potentiostat/galvanostat. The photoelectrochemical analysis was conducted in a three-electrode setup, with as-prepared samples, a Pt sheet and Ag/AgCl as the working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2S aqueous solution purged with N2 gas for 30 min. The photocurrents of samples illuminated by a 300 W ultraviolet lamp with or without 254 nm filter were recorded by a CHI660D electrochemical workstation.

In this study, all the first-principles density functional (DFT) calculations use the Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package (CASTEP) module, and the plane wave Vanderbilt type ultra-soft pseudopotential is close. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) function are used to explain the exchange correlation potential and electron–electron interaction. The kinetic cutoff energy of all calculations is 400 eV, and the convergence standard of self-consistent field is 1.0 × 10-5eV. For geometric optimization, the convergence criteria of energy variation and residual force are less than 1.0 × 10-5eV/atom and 0.1 eV/atom. For iron oxide, Brillouin zone is sampled in 2 × 2 × 2 glass samples with k-point grid geometry optimization. In addition, the geometric structures of different crystal planes of CQDs loaded Fe3O4 and α-Fe2O3 were calculated by using 1 × 1 × 2 Brillouin zone grid.

Fig. 1 a-g exhibits TEM and HR-TEM images of the as-prepared γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. It was observed that the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs was well-dispersed spherical-type composites with average diameter of 5–10 nm (Fig. 5a). The HR-TEM images (Fig. 1b-c) show that the CQDs were well introduced in the particles. The lattices spacing of the crystalline CQDs and γ-Fe2O3 were 0.22 and 0.32 nm, 0.252, 0.295 and 0.48, respectively, corresponding to the diffraction planes of CQDs (002) and (100), γ-Fe2O3(311), (220) and (113) [28]. Furthermore, the related EDX mapping analysis revealed a uniform elements distribution of C, O and Fe in the matrix of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs in (Fig. 1d-g), and the enriched distribution of C on the particles beyond the background implied that CQDs were successfully attached on the surface of γ-Fe2O3. The XRD spectra (Fig. 1h) shows that a new peak of CQDs at 18° (2θ) appeared in the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs sample, while increasing the CQDs loading ratio or calcination treatment would lead to the increase or disappearance of the peak, respectively (Fig. S1). FTIR examinations (Fig. 1i) presents the obvious occurrence of carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, and the related XPS results (Fig. 1j-k) indicates the strong interaction of surface C and O after the CQDs loading procedure. All above characterization results verified that CQDs have been successfully combined on the surface of γ-Fe2O3, which would lead to potential excellent photo-electrochemical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative degradation of SMX in different systems
ce:section.ce:para: Comparative degradation of SMX in the UV/iron oxides (IOs)/OA systems were investigated by using γ-Fe2O3, hydrothermal treated HT-γ-Fe2O3 (without addition of carbon dots precursor), mechanical mixture of CQDs/γ-Fe2O3 (CQDs&γ-Fe2O3), and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 a, either γ-Fe2O3 or HT-γ-Fe2O3exhibited initial lag degradation phases with OA under UV, and gradual decompositions of SMX began after the reaction time of 15 min. This phenomenon was consistent with previous studies that UV/IOs/OA systems would mainly occur homogeneous LMCT based on complexing dissolution of FeIII-OA species from the adopted natural or synthesized iron oxides [29–31]. On the contrary, the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system could achieve obviously significant SMX degradation from the reaction beginning and ca. 85 % of SMX could be decomposed at 15 min, hinting the occurrence of fast photochemical interfacial LMCT for ROS generation. Mechanical mixture of CQDs&γ-Fe2O3 would completely inhibit the SMX degradation, due to that the free CQDs played as a macromolecular organic substance preferentially attacked by generated ROS [32]. Besides, the simultaneous consumptions of OA occurred with the SMX degraded in the three UV/IOs/OA systems. It indicated that the complexed OA molecules (surface-bonded and aqueous) would be the main source in forming •OH via the pathway of C2O4 •− and CO2 •− [33]. As presented in the inset of Fig. 2a. the related consumption ratio of OA vs SMX (ΔOA/ΔSMX) were rather different. The UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system kept a constant ratio of about 4.0, while the other two systems have more higher values of above 10.0. This result suggested that the surface-bonded OA on the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs would be excited to directly generate ROS, instead of inducing homogeneous LMCT after the non-reductive dissolution [29]. Comparative mineralization of SMX in UV/iron oxides (IOs)/OA systems (Fig. S2), γ-Fe2O3/CQDs shows obvious advantages over HT- γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3.

Fig. 2b also exhibits the simultaneous time-dependent iron dissolution in the four UV/IOs/OA systems. It was interesting to note that the evolutions of ferrous and ferric ions were similar in the cases of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs and HT-γ-Fe2O3, showing relative rapid Fe2+ accumulation but Fe3+ trend of reducing the rise first. However, the accumulation of Fe2+ in the two systems of γ-Fe2O3 and CQDs&γ-Fe2O3 occurred only after 10 min, despite their Fe3+ evolutions were similar to the above two systems. Considering the related SMX degradation patterns (Fig. 2a), it could be concluded that non-reductive dissolution and accumulation of FeIII-OA species would be essential in the UV/IOs/OA systems to initialize homogeneous photochemical LMCT for oxidizing organics [29], while the surface of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs might occur rapid interfacial LMCT and generated ROS for the fast initial SMX degradation with the relevant moderate iron leaching.

The dissolution of iron ions and the consumption of OA in the UV/CQDs&γ-Fe2O3/OA system was monitored (Fig. S3). It can be observed that the decomposition of OA and dissolution of iron ions in the UV/CQDs&γ-Fe2O3/OA system was much slower than that in the CQDs/γ-Fe2O3/OA system. Thus, it can be concluded that the negligible degradation of SMX in the physically-mixed CQDs&γ-Fe2O3 could not only be attributed to the radical scavenger feature of CQDs, but also to the inferior photochemical performance of the CQDs&γ-Fe2O3 due to the negligible synergistic effect between CQDs and γ-Fe2O3.

Fig. 2c presents the time-dependent SMX degradation in UV/IOs/CQDs/OA systems using α-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3, Fe3O4 and γ-Fe2O3 with a CQDs doping ratio of 0.05 g/g, respectively. At 0.2 g/L of catalyst and 0.2 mM of OA, the effective SMX degradation in the four systems was achieved in a sequence of α-FeOOH/CQDs ≪α-Fe2O3/CQDs < Fe3O4/CQDs ≈ γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, which was just the opposite of that in the corresponding UV/IOs/OA systems [17]. As shown in Fig. S4, the dissolution of iron ions and the consumption of OA by adopting the α-Fe2O3/CQDs, Fe3O4/CQDs, α-FeOOH /CQDs, and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalysts were studied. The results show that a much slower OA decomposition rate and a much lower Fe2+ concentration was detected in the α-FeOOH/CQDs system. But they show a similar trend in the α-Fe2O3/CQDs, Fe3O4/CQDs, and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs systems. It suggested the surface structures of IOs related closely to their catalytic activities, but the introduction of CQDs would comprehensively accelerate and/or vary the interfacial reactions to different extent. In addition, several common organic ligands, such as citric acid, tartaric acid and EDTA were investigated for the SMX degradation in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/Ligand system. From Fig. 2d, it can be seen that a few amounts of SMX could be degraded in the cases of citric acid and tartaric acid, whereas addition of EDTA in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system would almost suppress the degradation. It could be attributed to the better photochemical activity of the iron-oxalate complexes which was obviously originated from the reductive/chelating dissolution of Fe(III)-OA species from the IOs surface sites [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the SMX degradation in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system
ce:section.ce:para: Important factors such as loading ratio of CQDs (0–0.08 mg/g), dosages of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs (0.05–2 g/L) and OA (0–0.3 mM), initial pH (2.0–5.0) and SMX concentration (5–20 mg/L), and reaction temperature (10–45 ℃) were investigated for their effects on the SMX degradation in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system. It was found that the SMX degradation pattern in all cases could be applied by the pseudo-first-order kinetic. As shown in Fig. 3 a, as the loading ratio of CQDs increased from 0 to 0.05 mg/g, the degradation kinetic constant (kobs) of SMX increased linearly at 0.1 g/L γ-Fe2O3/CQDs and 0.2 mM OA, while it would turn down as further increased the ratio to 0.08 mg/g. Obviously, loading CQDs was conducive to accumulate the photochemical generated electrons from γ-Fe2O3 for O2 activation, but excessive surface-bonded CQDs would occupy more active sites of γ-Fe2O3 and may retard the surface complexion of OA molecules for subsequent photochemical reactions. Fig. 3a also shows the effects of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs dosage on the degradation of SMX. When the initial OA was of 0.2 mM, 0.05 g/L of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs could reach the SMX degradation efficiency more than 70 % at 30 min. Increased the dosage to 0.1 g/L improved the kobs(SMX), but higher catalyst dosage would lead to gradual inhibitions in the SMX degradation. This could ascribe to the shading and light scattering effect which retarded the interfacial photochemical reactions [35].

Fig. 3b illustrates the effect of OA dosage on the SMX degradation. At a fixed γ-Fe2O3/CQDs of 0.1 g/L, increasing the OA dosage gradually from 0 to 0.2 mM would lead to rapid and stepwise improvements in the kobs(SMX), while 0.3 mM OA would lead to a little decrease in the SMX degradation. It suggested that amounts of the attached OA at the Fe sites on γ-Fe2O3/CQDs surface determined the overall photochemical reaction rate [36], and excessive OA would also compete with the generated ROS [37]. Besides, the best degradation efficiency was obtained in the case of pH 3.0, basically consistent with the reported UV/IOs/OA systems where solution pH greatly influenced the coordination morphology of OA with iron ions or solid surface iron, and strongly determined the number of active radical species [13]. Nevertheless, it was interesting to note that the SMX degradation in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system was effective at pH range of 2.0–4.0, and could still achieve about 20 % in the case of pH 5.0. This phenomenon was different from those UV/IOs/OA systems [38], suggesting that loading CQDs could enhance the photochemical in-situ oxidation performance based on γ-Fe2O3 and OA.

Fig. 3c illustrates the effects of initial SMX concentration and reaction temperature on the SMX degradation, respectively. Increased initial SMX concentration would lead to decrease in the kobs(SMX), but the total removal amount was gradually increased. It indicated that amount of generated ROS in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system was relatively constant, most depending on the number of surface activated sites of IOs for coordination of OA [39]. Furthermore, it was found that rising temperature was beneficial to the SMX degradation and the kobs(SMX) increased from 0.028 to 0.181 min−1 from 10 to 45 ℃. A positive linear correlation was observed between the ln(kobs(SMX)) and 1/T, where the Arrhenius activation energy (Ea) for the SMX degradation in the system could be calculated as 4.61 kJ mol−1. This low value suggested that the interfacial photochemical reactions on the surface of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs would be the rate-limited step for the ROS generation as well as SMX degradation in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system [40]. The relative time-dependent removal of SMX was shown in the Fig. S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical examinations of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs with OA or not
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electronic structures and reduction ability of the electrons, VB XPS of the γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs before and after 60 min pre-adsorption of OA were conducted and the results are shown in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that the valence band maximums (VBM) of γ-Fe2O3, γ-Fe2O3-OA, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs-OA were 1.41, 1.33, 1.24, and 1.45 eV, respectively. Fig. 4b illustrates the related UV/Vis-DRS examinations of the γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs experiencing 60 min pre-adsorption of OA or not. It was observed that the two γ-Fe2O3 samples have steep absorption edge in the range of visible light, whereas the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs-OA exhibited similar and high absorbance in full wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Then, the band gap energy (Eg) could be calculated as 1.90, 1.89, 1.45 and 1.56 eV, respectively (Inset of Fig. 4b), indicating that all of them were indirect semiconductors within the reported range of 1.4–2.0 eV [41]. The VBM and conduction band minimum (CBM) potentials of the above four samples can be comparatively presented in Fig. 4c. The Mott-Schottky spectra of the γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs before and after 60 min pre-adsorption of OA was provided in Fig. S6. Generally speaking, for n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential can be considered as the bottom position of the conduction band. [42] It was widely accepted that pristine γ-Fe2O3 possesses weak photocatalytic activity for SMX degradation because of its poor photogenerated carriers’ separation and transfer efficiency. The surface adsorption of OA may cause the positions of VB and CB of γ-Fe2O3 shifted more negatively, which could result in the photogenerated electrons more easily derived on the surface of γ-Fe2O3. The existence of CQDs may provide a low empty orbit for the CB of γ-Fe2O3, which could broaden the fully utilizing the energy from the solar spectrum [21] in addition to more effective electrons transfer. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs-OA could be related to its positive EVB potential. The photogenerated hole in the VB of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs -OA would be sufficient positive to drive the oxidization of HCO2 •− in oxalate to CO2 •− [43]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the loaded CQDs and adsorbed OA could induce significant synergistic effects in the photochemical IOs systems.

Attaching OA and/or CQDs could obviously improve the photocatalytic performance of the γ-Fe2O3. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the photocurrent density of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode under intermittent simulated UV irradiation was higher than that of the γ-Fe2O3, along with longer life span. It suggested that the photo-generated electrons in the γ-Fe2O3 part would be effectively transferred to the conductive CQDs part with higher surface area [44]. Interestingly, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs and γ-Fe2O3 with surface pre-adsorbed OA could exhibit rather higher photocurrent densities than their counter cases without OA. It was because that the photochemical reduction reactions of Fe(III)-complexes would be dominant instead of the direct photoactivation of γ-Fe2O3, and the former process was more efficient [29]. Moreover, the peak value of photocurrent was great higher for the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs-OA than that of the γ-Fe2O3-OA, and its time-dependent pattern was also more stable in the whole lifespan of 0–200 s, suggesting the occurrence of initial effective interfacial photochemical reactions based on the surface-complexed OA species. However, the pattern of the γ-Fe2O3-OA was of a gradual growth type as the irradiation time elapsed, hinting that the slow non-reduction dissolution of FeIII-OA would be dominant to induce the subsequent homogeneous photochemical reactions [29].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also adopted to investigate the migration of charge carriers in the above four catalysts. Fig. 5b shows that the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode possessed a decreased arc at high frequency under UV irradiation as compared to that of the γ-Fe2O3 electrode, manifesting much more efficient transfer of charge carriers obtained over the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. With the 60 min pre-adsorption of OA upon the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, the related arc further decreased significantly. It revealed that that the adsorbed OA on the γ-Fe2O3 surface could occur excellent interfacial charge carriers transfer [45], favoring the rapid generation of ROS in the initial reaction phase in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system (Fig. 1a). Cyclic voltammogram test was further adopted to verify the effect of surface-loaded CQDs and/or pre-adsorbed OA on the electrochemical performance of the γ-Fe2O3. As presented in Fig. 5c, the loading of CQDs apparently improved the CV current of γ-Fe2O3, suggesting a more drastic redox reaction occurred on the surface of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. Most importantly, in the two cases of OA pre-adsorption, loading CQDs could significantly enlarge the CV current as compared to the pristine γ-Fe2O3, indicating that the CQDs and OA could work in a synergetic manner for improving electron conduction efficiently. Furthermore, with CQDs loading, the positive potential of oxidation peak was lower, which shifts from 0.97 V to 0.94 V. The positive potential of reduction peak was higher, which shifts from 0.09 V to 0.26 V. With OA adsorption on γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, positive potential of oxidation peak and reduction peak becomes lower obviously. Oxidation peak at a significantly lower positive potential represents easier oxidation, which means that the catalyst is easier to obtain electrons with CQDs loading or OA adsorption. Reduction peak at a significantly lower positive potential represents easier reduction, which means that the catalyst is easier to lost electrons with OA adsorption. Carbon quantum dots act as storage containers of electrons, increasing ability of the γ-Fe2O3 for obtain and store electrons.

FTIR analysis was performed to explore the interactions between CQDs and γ-Fe2O3 as well as OA and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 5d, the γ-Fe2O3 sample had a characteristic peak at 540 cm−1 associated with stretching vibration of Fe-O bonds, while the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs presented four characteristic peaks at 1631, 1413, 1088 and 540 cm−1, which could be related to stretching vibrations of –C=O, -O-C=O, -O-C-C and Fe-O, respectively. It indicated that CQDs were successfully attached on the surface of γ-Fe2O3 since the former three functional groups are abundant in the structure of the glucose precursor [24]. In-situ ATR-FTIR was conducted to clarify the iron-oxalate species on the surface of γ-Fe2O3, as shown in Fig. S7, the adsorption of oxalate on the surface of γ-Fe2O3 led to the appearance of four characteristic peaks at 1709, 1687, 1407, 1309 cm−1, which corresponding to the formation of the bidentate mononuclear inner sphere iron-oxalate complex (i.e., ≡FeIII(C2O4)n]3-2n) [19,46,47]. Moreover, pre-adsorption of OA upon the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs would cause similar but more strong characteristic peaks to that of the γ-Fe2O3-OA, suggesting OA molecules would be complexed at the γ-Fe2O3 surface of the composite due to the blue or red shifts of the peaks of the three carboxyl bonds of CQDs.

Fig. 6 shows the XPS characterization results of the above four samples. There were two distinct peaks with binding energies (BE) of 529.86 and 530.84 eV for the O 1 s core level (Fig. 6a), which can be assigned to Fe-O and C=O, respectively [48]. The content of C=O species on the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs surface was significantly higher than that of the γ-Fe2O3, implying that the CQDs have been successfully bonded on the surface of γ-Fe2O3. In addition, the peak position of the C=O shifted to higher BE in the cases of γ-Fe2O3-OA and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs-OA, indicating that OA could provide its lone pair of electrons to form an inner-sphere complex with the surface iron species. Meanwhile, C 1 s peaks of C-C (284.8 eV), C-O-C (286.32 eV) and O-C=O (288.5 eV) bonds were observed in the cases of γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, whereas the signals of C-O-C disappeared in their counter cases of OA-pre-adsorption. This indicated that OA was successfully complexed on the surface of the γ-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. Furthermore, two Fe 2p XPS peaks of the neat γ-Fe2O3 located at 711.3 and 709.99 eV could be assigned to Fe(III) and Fe(II), respectively [24,45]. Loading CQDs would cause obvious increase in the surface Fe(II) proportion, suggesting CQDs would be chemically loaded on the γ-Fe2O3. OA pre-adsorption would lead to an apparent decrease in the proportion of surface Fe(III) upon the γ-Fe2O3, whereas affect slightly with respect to the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. This finding implied that OA pre-adsorption of pristine IOs would lead to non-reductive dissolution of ferric, but the loaded CQDs may prevent this process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic study of the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system
Determination of the reactive oxidative species
ce:section.ce:para:
ROS generation in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system was examined by EPR and DMPO was used as the spin trap to capture •OH and CO2 •− in the water system, as well as O2 •− in the methanol system, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 a, two series of signal peaks of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-CO2 •− appeared at 10 min in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA (water) system, and the •OH signal became stronger gradually but CO2 •− disappeared as the reaction time further elapsed to 20 and 30 min. Meanwhile, apparent accumulation of O2 •− could be observed at 30 min in the methanol system. This result indicated that •OH would be the main ROS for the SMX degradation in the system, which was closely related to the generated O2 •− that originated from the photochemical reduction of surface and/or homogeneous coordinated OA molecules such as Fe2(C2O4)3 3– and [≡FeIII(C2O4)n]3-2n, as depicted in Eqs 1–4 [30,31]. However, it was noted that the fate of CO2 •− was rather different from our previous studies of UV/IOs/OA systems, in which CO2 •− was detected and accumulated in the late homogeneous reaction period [17,29]. This suggested that the interfacial photochemical LMCT of the surface-bonded FeIII-OA complexes would be effective from the reaction beginning, transferring the generated electrons from C2O4 •− and CO2 •− to O2 for generation of O2 •−.
(1) Fe(III)[(C2O4)n]3−2n (surface or bulk) + hv → Fe(II)C2O4 +(n − 1)C2O4 2− +C2O4 •−
(2) C2O4 •−→ CO2 •− + CO2
(3) CO2 •− + O2 → CO2 + O2 •−
(4) C2O4 •− + O2 → 2CO2 + O2 •−
(5) O2 •− ↔ HO2 • (pH dependent)
(6) O2 •−/HO2 • + H+ → H2O2 + O2
(7) O2 •−/HO2 • + H+ +Fe2+ → H2O2 + O2 + Fe3+
(8) Fe(II)[(C2O4)n]3-2n + H2O2 → Fe(III)[(C2O4)n]3-2n + •OH + OH−

Scavenging experiments using 100 mM of tert-butyl alcohol, ethanol, methanol and p-benzoquinone were further conducted to check the effect on the SMX degradation in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system, respectively, since the former three scavengers could screen bulk as well as interfacial •OH, and the last could capture O2 •− [49]. Fig. 7b exhibits that the SMX degradation would be almost completely inhibited by all the four scavengers, revealing that O2 •− and •OH would be predominant for the SMX degradation in UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system. Besides, the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system with N2 sparging caused strong inhibition in the SMX degradation, but could still remain about 10.5 % of removal efficiency. This phenomenon was different from the UV/IOs/OA systems where the degradations were completely suppressed under the anoxic circumstances [50]. It was because that O2 would be the critical electron acceptor for the photo-activation of both homogeneous and interfacial FeIII-OA complexes, but the loaded CQDs would pre-adsorb molecular oxygen providing effective occurrence of the initial internal LMCT for the interfacial ROS generation [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evolutions of dissolved iron in the different comparative systems
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 a-b compare time-dependent evolutions of the dissolved iron in the UV or dark γ-Fe2O3/CQDs systems with N2 sparging or air atmosphere, as well as OA or not, respectively. In the dark systems, direct proton dissolution of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs were similar and slow under N2 sparging and air atmosphere, with about 0.7 mg/L iron released at 30 min and high proportions of soluble Fe(II). The introduction of OA could bring more amounts of dissolved iron (∼2.0 mg/L) under the two atmospheres, but soluble Fe(III) would be dominant due to the preferential complexing dissolution of ferric from the surface of γ-Fe2O3. UV irradiation of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs suspensions under either N2 or air did not cause more amounts of dissolved iron, but led to much higher proportions of soluble Fe(II) which was ascribed to the photo-reduction of Fe(III). However, the evolution of dissolved iron would be rather different in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs systems under N2 or air. Only Fe(II) with accumulation of 2.5 mg/L was observed in the N2-UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs system, indicating photochemical reductive dissolution of the OA-complexed surface Fe(III) occurred on the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs in the absence of oxygen [52]. Meanwhile, Fe(III) was the main aqueous iron species in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs (air) system in the initial 0–5 min and then Fe(II) turned to predominant, suggesting occurrence of non-reductive dissolution of Fe(III) initially [53].

To further distinguish the role of heterogenous/homogeneous iron-based photochemical reactions, comparative SMX degradation in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system was conducted with 30 min pre-adsorption of OA under dark or not. As presented in Fig. 8c, it was found that SMX was degraded rapidly in both systems as UV introduced, although concentrations of the dissolved iron were greatly high in the pre-adsorption system. Besides, the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system could achieve efficient and stable SMX degradation in six repeated runs (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. S10, the characterization results including XRD, XPS, FT-IR and Raman of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs composite before and after photochemical tests have been provided. It can be seen that the crystalline structure and surface composition of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs composite was rather stable in the reaction process. This result indicated the occurrence of effective interfacial photochemical LMCT on the surface of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, which would be mainly responsible for the ROS generation and SMX degradation. According to the intermediates and previous studies [54], the proposed pathways of SMZ degradation in UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system was shown in Fig. S9. The sulfonamide bond cleavage first, followed by heterocyclic hydroxylation cleavage.

In addition, the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system could be capable of utilizing O2 more effective than the UV/γ-Fe2O3/OA system. Under the anaerobic circumstance, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs system could still obtain SMX degradation efficiency of 28 % at 30 min, while the γ-Fe2O3/OA system achieved only marginal degradation (<5%) (Fig. 8d). It was because that the loaded CQDs could capture and enrich O2 on its surface as storage in the air [55], initializing and maintaining the photochemical interfacial LMCT on the surface of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. Meanwhile, OA was also simultaneously consumed gradually (Inset in the Fig. 8d) and a total amount of 0.04 mM at 30 min was detected which associated to 0.01 mM SMX degradation. This indicated the LMCT, ROS generation on the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs surface would be still efficient even in the absence of DO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system based on the excited surface LMCT
ce:section.ce:para: According to the three detected γ-Fe2O3 crystalline planes (220, 113 and 311) in the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst, DFT calculations were conducted to study the combination configuration of the CQDs and γ-Fe2O3. The result is shown in Fig. S11-S12, it can be seen that CQDs would be preferential to form C-O bonds at the surface O sites of the γ-Fe2O3, with similar bond energies of 1.843 (220 plane), 1.805 (113 plane) and 1.823 (311 plane), also evidenced by the characteristic examinations of FTIR and XPS (Figs. 1 and 6). The formed C-O bonds could favor the photogenerated electrons in the γ-Fe2O3 component transferring rapidly and enriching to the CQDs component, leading to significant improvement in the photochemical interfacial LMCT [56]. Meanwhile, the surface Fe sites would remain exposure to OA chelation, assuring the quantum efficiency of the surface-bound Fe(III)-OA complexes [57].

Furthermore, a mechanistic scheme for the O2 activation and SMX degradation in the UV/γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system could be proposed as presented in Fig. 9 . Under the surface complexion of OA and UV irradiation, the specific structure of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalysts could excite the surface LMCT and single-electron reduction of O2 efficiently [58–60], avoiding the electron-hole recombination in UV/IOs systems and Fe2+-OA dominated homogeneous LMCT in the UV/IOs/OA systems [11]. In detailed, OA would be first adsorbed on the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs surface to form a bidentate mononuclear complex of [≡FeIII(C2O4)n]3-2n, followed by the efficient UV-excited LMCT and fast internal transmission of electrons to the loaded CQDs for activating its surface adsorbed O2 to generate O2 •−. Meanwhile, the photochemically generated surface FeII-OA species (Eq. (1)) would react with the generated O2 •− (Eq. (7)) or H2O2 (Eqs. (6) and (8)) to produce •OH, leading to rapid SMX degradation and non-reductive leaching of aqueous FeIII(C2O4)n 2-2n, even under visible light irradiation (Fig. S13). Additionally, the homogeneous photochemical LMCT and Fenton like reactions between bulk FeIIIOA and FeII-OA species (homogeneous iron cycle) would also occur to generate CO2 •−, O2 •−and •OH, providing another contribution to the oxidative degradation of SMX.

The aim of this study was to excite and utilize the critical interfacial LMCT process in common heterogeneous UV/IOs/OA system by successfully modifying maghemite by easily prepared CQDs of excellent photoelectrochemical properties. Characterizations of the synthesized γ-Fe2O3/CQDs indicated the critical role of the loaded CQDs in driving the occurrence of efficient LMCT upon the surface FeIII-OA species and fast internal electron transmission to activate adsorbed O2 on the CQDs surface. O2 •− and •OH would be predominant for SMX degradation in the UV-γ-Fe2O3/CQDs/OA system, revealing the interfacial radical reactions based on the consecutive single-electron transfers from complexed OA to O2. A comprehensive mechanism could be therefore proposed for the SMX degradation in the system, integrating the interfacial photochemical LMCT and fast reduction of CQDs-adsorbed O2, heterogeneous iron cycle and Fenton-like reactions, as well as the additional secondary homogeneous LMCT. The result of this study is expected to provide a new and feasible method towards efficient utilization of photogenerated electrons in natural iron oxides/oxalic acid systems, broadening their applications in environmental decontamination.
